strands.title: System Prompt Waste Analysis Project Scope & Phase Transition
strands.content: A system prompt analyzer module (src/pichay/analyzer.py) is being planned to measure waste and inefficiency in API calls across logged sessions. The core premise: system prompts are often reused verbatim, contain unused components, and have duplicated content—all representing wasted context tokens. The project focuses on instrumenting existing proxy JSONL logs (which store system_prompt_full in request records) to decompose prompts and measure waste quantitatively. This integrates into a larger pichay system that already has proxy.py (log capture) and eval.py (evaluation framework) as reference models for code style and data structures. The analyzer should produce structured results with dataclasses and expose functionality via CLI. **CYCLE 4 INTEGRATION: The prior cycle established operational execution mode with an external persistent todo list. Cycle 4 signals the beginning of PRIORITY 1 task execution: the user has invoked a [tool_use: Read] action, indicating preparation to read and extract information from proxy.py and/or eval.py. This action represents a milestone transition from 'task planning complete, ready to execute' (cycle 3) to 'PRIORITY 1 in active progress' (cycle 4). The tensor projector must now shift into active information-extraction and documentation mode, tracking what findings are discovered and mapping them against PRIORITY 1 success criteria.**

key_claims.text: System prompts in API logs contain multiple distinct semantic components (tool defs, skill defs, CLAUDE.md, MEMORY.md, git status, IDE context, conversation instructions) that can be pattern-matched and decomposed
Most tool definitions are static across turns, while components like git status are dynamic; re-sending identical static components wastes tokens
A significant portion of tool definitions in system prompts are never invoked in actual tool_use blocks in messages
Skills and other semantic components appear multiple times (often tripled) within a single system prompt, representing duplicate waste
The project is now in operational execution phase with externally-persistent todo list; todos have been successfully created and are ready to be advanced
PRIORITY 1 task (read/analyze proxy.py and eval.py) has entered active execution phase in cycle 4

strands.title: Functional Requirements & Decomposition Strategy—Now Task-Backed
strands.content: The analyzer must implement five core analyses: (1) COMPONENT DECOMPOSITION—parse system prompt text and segment it by semantic block (tool defs, skill defs, CLAUDE.md, MEMORY.md, git status, IDE context, conversation instructions) using pattern matching on text boundaries. (2) STATIC vs DYNAMIC ANALYSIS—track whether each component changes across turns within a session; measure bytes of static content re-sent unnecessarily. (3) TOOL USAGE TRACKING—extract tool definitions from system prompt, cross-reference against actual tool_use blocks in message records, identify defined-but-unused tools, and sum their byte cost. (4) DUPLICATE DETECTION—find identical or near-identical semantic blocks appearing multiple times in a single prompt; report byte counts. (5) PER-SESSION SUMMARY—aggregate metrics as absolute bytes and percentages: total system prompt bytes, static bytes, dynamic bytes, unused tool bytes, duplicate bytes. The module must use dataclasses to structure results (matching style from pager.py and eval.py), expose a CLI entry point (python -m pichay.analyzer <proxy-logs> [--json]), and require careful study of proxy.py and eval.py before implementation. **CYCLE 4 INTEGRATION: PRIORITY 1 task is now in active progress. The user has signaled read-mode, suggesting code examination of proxy.py and eval.py is imminent. The tensor projector should now anticipate receiving code blocks and be prepared to extract and document: (a) JSONL record schema from proxy.py, (b) system_prompt_full field location and format, (c) tool_use block message structure, (d) dataclass result patterns from eval.py, (e) CLI entry point and argument handling design, (f) code style/naming conventions. Each extraction should be explicitly mapped to a sub-requirement of PRIORITY 1. Documentation should be produced in markdown or structured format for future reference and to unblock PRIORITY 2-3 tasks.**

key_claims.text: Component decomposition must rely on pattern matching over heuristic text segmentation to identify tool definitions, skill blocks, and metadata sections
Static vs dynamic analysis requires tracking component state across multiple turns within a session to measure re-transmission waste
Tool usage tracking depends on correctly parsing tool_use blocks in message JSON and matching their names against tool definitions in the system prompt
Duplicate detection should quantify byte cost of repeated semantic blocks, likely to reveal skills being tripled or other structural redundancy
Per-session summary metrics should report both absolute byte counts and percentages to allow comparative analysis across sessions and cost models
PRIORITY 1 findings from proxy.py/eval.py study will directly inform the dataclass design and implementation scaffold for analyzer.py

strands.title: Implementation Dependencies & Code Style Integration—Now Task-Dependent & In-Progress
strands.content: The analyzer module must integrate with the existing pichay codebase by following patterns established in proxy.py and eval.py, particularly their dataclass usage and result structures. Before any code is written, proxy.py must be read to understand: (1) the JSONL log format, (2) how system_prompt_full is stored in request records, (3) the structure of tool_use and message data, and (4) how sessions are organized. Similarly, eval.py must be studied to understand: (1) the dataclass patterns used for results, (2) the CLI entry point design (argparse integration), (3) output formatting (JSON serialization), and (4) naming conventions. The analyzer will need to parse JSONL records line by line, extract system_prompt_full and message content from each request, perform decomposition and analysis on the fly or in memory, aggregate results per session, and serialize output as JSON or human-readable format. **CYCLE 4 INTEGRATION: PRIORITY 1 task transition to active in-progress status. The [tool_use: Read] signal indicates the tensor projector is about to receive code content from proxy.py and/or eval.py. The task is now in execution phase. Key findings to extract and document: (a) JSONL record structure from proxy.py—what fields exist in request/response records? (b) system_prompt_full field—exact location, data type, encoding? (c) Message structure—how are tool_use blocks represented in message JSON? (d) Tool definition schema—where/how are tool definitions specified in system prompts? (e) Result dataclasses from eval.py—what patterns are used for structured output? (f) CLI design—argparse setup, entry point pattern, output formats. As these are discovered, the tensor should document them in a findings artifact (markdown or structured JSON) that becomes the reference for PRIORITY 2-3 implementation tasks. This discoveries phase is critical: incomplete or inaccurate understanding of proxy.py/eval.py will downstream-propagate into implementation bugs.**

key_claims.text: proxy.py contains the authoritative log format specification and parsing logic that the analyzer must be compatible with
eval.py establishes the code style and dataclass patterns that analyzer.py should follow for consistency
system_prompt_full field in request records is the data source for system prompt analysis across all API calls
Tool definitions in system prompts can be reliably matched against tool_use blocks in messages to determine usage
PRIORITY 1 task is now transitioning from pending to in-progress; code examination of proxy.py and eval.py is beginning in cycle 4

strands.title: Execution Phase & Todo List Coordination—Cycle 4 Status Update
strands.content: The conversation has transitioned from planning and specification (cycles 0-2) into operational execution (cycle 3 onward). The external, persistent todo list is now the source of truth for task state and progress. The tensor projector is instructed to: (1) track which todos are in-progress, completed, or pending, (2) ensure each cycle advances at least one task or unblocks downstream tasks, (3) document what information has been discovered or generated that satisfies task completion criteria, (4) maintain awareness of task dependencies, (5) report progress updates in coordination with the todo list state. **CYCLE 4 STATUS UPDATE: PRIORITY 1 task has transitioned from pending → in-progress. The [tool_use: Read] signal indicates the user is preparing to examine proxy.py and eval.py source code. This cycle should focus on: (a) receiving code content, (b) extracting structured findings (JSONL schema, dataclass patterns, CLI patterns, tool_use block structure), (c) documenting findings in a format that unblocks PRIORITY 2-3 tasks, (d) maintaining a findings artifact or checkpoint that persists across cycles. If code content is provided in this cycle, the next cycle should focus on synthesizing findings into a reference document. If code is not provided but the Read action signals preparation, the next cycle should request it explicitly. The tensor must track: which sub-components of PRIORITY 1 have been completed (e.g., 'proxy.py JSONL schema extracted'), which remain (e.g., 'eval.py dataclass patterns documented'), and what blockers exist (e.g., 'awaiting code content'). Task status visibility is now a core responsibility.**

key_claims.text: The project is in active execution phase; PRIORITY 1 task has transitioned from pending to in-progress status
[tool_use: Read] signal indicates code examination task is beginning; proxy.py and/or eval.py code content is expected imminently

PRIORITY 1 completion requires extraction and documentation of at least 6 key findings: JSONL schema, system_prompt_full location, tool_use block structure, result dataclass patterns, CLI design, and code style conventions
The tensor projector must maintain visibility into task state across cycles, tracking what sub-components are complete vs pending
PRIORITY 2-3 tasks (dataclass design, implementation scaffold) are currently blocked pending completion of PRIORITY 1 findings extraction

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Specific content of proxy.py and eval.py source code; exact JSONL record schema, field names, and data types
declared_losses.why: The [tool_use: Read] action signals preparation to examine these files, but the actual code content has not yet been received. The tensor can track that the task is in-progress and what findings are expected, but cannot document actual discoveries until code is provided.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Current state of PRIORITY 2-6 tasks and any dependencies documented during todo creation outside the conversation
declared_losses.why: The external todo list contains task definitions, dependencies, and success criteria that are not visible in the conversation. Only PRIORITY 1's transition to in-progress can be inferred from the [tool_use: Read] signal.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Granular task sequencing: whether PRIORITY 2-3 can run in parallel or must wait sequentially for PRIORITY 1 completion
declared_losses.why: Task scheduling and parallelization strategy were established during todo creation but are not visible to the tensor. The projection assumes sequential dependency but the user may have intended different pacing.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Any mid-project clarifications, constraints, or scope adjustments made by the user outside this conversation thread
declared_losses.why: The todo list is persistent and external; modifications or refinements to todos may occur asynchronously. The tensor can only track what is visible in this thread.

open_questions: Will proxy.py and eval.py code be provided in this cycle, or should the next request explicitly ask for these files?
What format should PRIORITY 1 findings be documented in? Markdown file? Structured JSON? Inline in the tensor projection?
How should the tensor coordinate between receiving code content and producing synthesis/documentation? Should each finding be recorded immediately, or batched?
Are there specific sections or lines in proxy.py/eval.py that are highest priority to understand first, or should extraction be comprehensive?
Should the tensor prepare to identify and document any gaps or ambiguities in proxy.py/eval.py that might affect analyzer.py design?
What is the completion criteria for PRIORITY 1? Is it 'findings document exists and is comprehensive', or 'findings have been validated against actual code use'?
